coredata.dc:title: A design strategy of ratiometric probe based on dual-colored carbon dots for phosphate detection
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Xiaolin
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: Establishment of a rapid, sensitive, and selective quantitative assay of food additives was of great significance in food safety evaluation. In this study, a novel ratiomentric probe (B/Y-CDs) based on dual-colored carbon dots was developed to detect phosphates (Pi) in frozen seafoods. With the aid of the strong coordination ability of Tb3+ and electrostatic attraction, the nanoprobe (B/Y-CDs) was constructed composed of yellow emissive terbium-doped carbon dots (Y-CDs) and the blue emissive carbon dots (B-CDs). Consequently, the blue fluorescence signal was significantly quenched based on Förster resonance energy transfer. Upon the addition of Pi, the stronger coordination capacity with Tb3+ would make the Y-CDs prefer to combine with Pi, causing the desorption of B/Y-CDs, thus recovering the blue fluorescence. Meanwhile, Y-CDs fluorescence remains stable, presenting the ratiometric detection of Pi. The analytical potential of the probe was evaluated and a good linear range from 0.5 to 12 μM was achieved. The whole analysis process could be accomplished within 15 min. In brief, this study offered a new design strategy of ratiometric probe for rapidly and sensitively detecting Pi in seafoods, which demonstrated potential application value in food safety evaluation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphate
Rare earth
Carbon dots
Ratiometric probe
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Pi in real food samples
The optical properties and photostability of B/Y-CDs
Testing of Pi in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Zhefeng fan, xiaolin tian reports financial support, administrative support, and equipment, drugs, or supplies were provided by Shanxi Normal University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of B-CDs (Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution histogram of B-CDs); (b) TEM image of Y-CDs (Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution histogram of Y-CDs); (c) FTIR spectra; (d) Zeta potentials of B-CDs and Y-CDs; (e) XPS survey spectra of B-CDs and Y-CD and deconvoluted (f) Elemental spectrum of Tb3d in Y-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra (Inset: photograph of B-CDs, Y-CDs, B/Y-CDs and B/Y-CDs + Pi under 365 nm UV lamp from left to right); (c) CIE coordinates of B-CDs, Y-CDs, B/Y-CDs and B/Y-CDs + Pi.
(a) The influence of pH on the B/Y-CDs probe; (b) The photostability of the B/Y-CDs irradiated by xenon lamp for different amounts of time.
(a) UV–vis absorption of Y-CDs (red line) and fluorescence emission of B-CDs (dark line); (b) Fluorescence lifetime curves of B-CDs and B-CDs in B/Y-CDs.
(a) Optimization of the ratio of B-CDs to Y-CDs; (b) Effect of pH values on the experimental results; (c) Effect of reaction time on experimental results.
(a) Variation of the fluorescence signal of B/Y-CDs with different Pi concentrations; (b) Plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F550/F440) against Pi concentration.
The influence of other ions and food ingredients on the probe (black bars) and the detecting results (red bars).
Schematic illustration of the fabrication of B/Y-CDs and detection mechanism for Pi.
Comparison of B/Y-CDs and the standard method for the detection of Pi.

head.ce:title: A design strategy of ratiometric probe based on dual-colored carbon dots for phosphate detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030031, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030031, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030031, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030031, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Establishment of a rapid, sensitive, and selective quantitative assay of food additives was of great significance in food safety evaluation. In this study, a novel ratiomentric probe (B/Y-CDs) based on dual-colored carbon dots was developed to detect phosphates (Pi) in frozen seafoods. With the aid of the strong coordination ability of Tb3+ and electrostatic attraction, the nanoprobe (B/Y-CDs) was constructed composed of yellow emissive terbium-doped carbon dots (Y-CDs) and the blue emissive carbon dots (B-CDs). Consequently, the blue fluorescence signal was significantly quenched based on Förster resonance energy transfer. Upon the addition of Pi, the stronger coordination capacity with Tb3+ would make the Y-CDs prefer to combine with Pi, causing the desorption of B/Y-CDs, thus recovering the blue fluorescence. Meanwhile, Y-CDs fluorescence remains stable, presenting the ratiometric detection of Pi. The analytical potential of the probe was evaluated and a good linear range from 0.5 to 12 μM was achieved. The whole analysis process could be accomplished within 15 min. In brief, this study offered a new design strategy of ratiometric probe for rapidly and sensitively detecting Pi in seafoods, which demonstrated potential application value in food safety evaluation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphates, one of commonly used food additive categories with a high dosage, are utilized extensively in the processing of aquatic products (especially frozen seafoods) as a crucial quality improver and nutritional enhancer [1]. They are indispensable in aquatic products for a number of reasons, as they may be used to improve the water holding capacity, effectively inhibit the oxidation of fat, and maintain the natural color and flavor of seafoods [2]. However, excess phosphate intake in humans is closely related to various clinical diseases such as hyperphosphatemia, rickets, Fanconi syndrome, and hyperthyroidism [3–5]. In order to preserve the food safety and safeguard the residents’ physical health, it is essential to monitor the phosphate concentration. According to the European Food Safety Authority and the National Food Safety Standards of China, the use limit of phosphate additive levels in frozen aquatic products is 5.0 g/kg (calculated by orthophosphate ions, PO4 3−, abbreviated as Pi) [6,7]. Common phosphate additives in foods are phosphates, pyrophosphates, hexametaphosphates, trimetaphosphates, and tripolyphosphates. To detect total phosphorus in actual sample testing, other forms of phosphate additives can be transformed to Pi through pre-treatment. Thus far, numerous methods have been developed for quantitative analysis of Pi, such as colorimetric methods [2,8], fluorescence analysis [9–11], chromatographic [12], electrochemical [13,14], and enzyme biosensors [15] et al. Among them, fluorescence analysis methods have been demonstrated to show higher sensitivity in quantitative and qualitative analysis. Considering phosphates additives are widely used in food industry, developing new sensing strategies for Pi detection is still highly desired.

As a kind of emerging efficient material, Carbon dots (CDs) have a size less than 10 nm with attractive properties like chemical inertness, high quantum fluorescence yield, and water solubility [16–19]. Based on these, CDs were also used to design sensors for the detection of Pi [20–22]. But in these applications, the majority of the sensors rely on a single, variable-intensity photoluminescence signal and are not sensitive enough to the target's color fluctuations, which precludes their widespread use in the detection of actual samples [23]. Because quantification of the target analyte using only the emission intensity at a wavelength may be influenced by target concentration-independent experimental or physiological factors, such as light scattering by the sample matrix, excitation source fluctuation, particular microenvironment around probes and local concentration variation of probes [24]. In response to this limitation, dual-emission ratiometric schemes have been designed and proposed to mitigate the effects of nonspecific background. Ideally, ratiometric measurement usually records two or more analyte-induced signal fluctuations to realize self-calibration of signal intensity, where one signal can act as an “internal standard” for normalizing the other signal [25,26]. Besides, an ‘‘off-on” switch typically provides a higher signal-to-noise ratio than an ‘‘on-off” switch [27]. To facilitate more accurate and reliable quantitation of Pi sensing, an ‘‘off-on” ratiometric probe based on CDs is worth exploring.

Although CDs show great potential in sensing applications, the low quantum yields of pristine CDs and the limited effective sensing sites on their surfaces lead to poor performance in terms of selectivity and sensitivity during fluorescence analysis [28]. Numerous efforts have been made to further improve the photoluminescence performance and enlarge application scope of CDs, and doping and surface modification are considered as the two primary methods to tune the photoluminescence properties [29]. Especially, doping relatively easily changes the electronic structure in relation to their band gap with relative ease, giving CDs new inherent qualities that make them better suited for analytical uses such as determining pH, solvent, ions, biological, and chemical small molecules [30]. Metal heteroatoms have more electrons and vacant orbitals than non-metallic heteroatoms, which makes it easier to modify the charge density on the surface of CDs. Additionally, the chelation of metal ions makes chemical interactions with the amino and carboxyl groups in CDs more advantageous [31]. Rare earth (RE) ions are widely used to dope CDs due to their strong coordination capabilities and facile interaction with surface functional groups on CDs, providing specific binding sites for targets, such as Pi.

Herein, we proposed a ratiometric strategy for the sensitive and rapid detection of Pi by constructing a self-assembling fluorescent nanoprobe (B/Y-CDs). The fabrication of the ratiometric probe was mainly based on two powerful findings: on the one hand, when B-CDs were mixed with Y-CDs, the fluorescence of B-CDs (a donor) was partially quenched by Y-CDs (an acceptor) based on the Förster energy resonance transfer (FRET); on the other hand, in the presence of Pi, due to the stronger coordination ability of Tb3+ with Pi, the FRET process was interrupted, then the blue fluorescence was recovered, while the yellow fluorescence remained unaffected as a stable internal standard, thus realizing the ratiometric detection of Pi (Scheme 1 ). The applied method was facile and efficient for quantitative detection of Pi in frozen seafood samples.

o-Phenylenediamine (C6H8N2), terbium nitrate (Tb(NO3)3·6H2O), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) and diethylenetriamine (DETA, C6H12N2) were supplied from Shanghai Macklin Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O) and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium nitrite (NaNO2), potassium bromide (KBr), and ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) were purchased from Shanghai Guoyao Chemical Reagent Company, Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and other salts were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagent Company, Ltd. All the reagents were of analytical grade, and the ultrapure water was utilized throughout the entire work.
The employed instruments in this experiment can be seen in the “supplementary data”.

B-CDs: citric acid (1 g, 5.21 mM) and DETA (350 μL, 0.336 g, 0.32 mM) were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water. The homogeneous solution was then sealed in a 50 mL of stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon liner for 5 h at 200 °C. After natural cooling, a membrane filter (0.22 μm) and a dialysis bag (MW:1000) were successively used to pure the reaction solution. Finally, the liquid in the dialysis bag was treated by freeze-drying and the dried powders were denoted as B-CDs [32].

Y-CDs: o-phenylenediamine (0.3 g, 0.092 mM) and Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (0.3 g, 0.022 mM) were dissolved in 30 mL ethanol, and the homogeneous solution was sealed in a 50 mL of stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon liner for 12 h at 180 °C.The reaction solution was then dried under vacuum at 40 °C until the ethanol evaporated, and the dried product was dissolved with 30 mL of ultrapure water. After natural cooling, a membrane filter (0.22 μm) and a dialysis bag (MW:1000) were successively used to pure the reaction solution. Finally, the liquid in the dialysis bag was treated by freeze-drying and the dried powders were denoted as Y-CDs. The synthesis of yellow emissive carbon dots without terbium doping was similar to the above steps except that terbium nitrate was removed from the precursors [33].
The optimization of dosage of terbium nitrate in the synthesis method can be seen in the “supplementary data”.

Quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) dissolved in 0.10 M H2SO4 as a reference material of B-CDs and Y-CDs [34]. Reference material solution and the CDs solutions were prepared such that their optical absorption values were in the range of 0–0.1. The corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded, and the following equation was used to calculate the QY:
Q Y =
Q Y
R
( I /
I R
)
(
A R
/ A )
( η /
η R
)
Where the subscript R represents the reference data; QY is the abbreviation for quantum yield; I refers to the integral area of fluorescence emission intensity; A indicates the absorbance; η is the refractive index of the solvent.

In a general procedure, 20 μL B-CDs solution (1 mg/mL) and 80 μL Y-CDs solution (13 mg/mL), as well as different concentrations of Pi, were added into a 10 mL cuvette and fixed to 5.0 mL with Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH = 7). After 15 min reaction, the emission spectra of the solutions were tested under 365 nm excitation. Then, the average value of F550/F440 was obtained as the output signal, where F550 and F440 indicate the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 550 nm and 440 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Pi in real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of B/Y-CD probe, three kinds of frozen seafoods (frozen shrimp, frozen fish and frozen squid) which were collected from the local supermarket were used as real samples for determination of Pi. 2 g of chopped samples and appropriate amount of water were ground into paste using a mortar and a pestle. Then sulfuric acid solution was added dropwise to adjust the pH to less than 1. Subsequently, the sample solutions were placed on an electric stove and heated and boiled for 30 min, during which time water was added to maintain the volume of the solution. After cooling, sodium hydroxide solution was added to adjust the pH of the solution to neutral. Finally, the solutions were filtered several times by using 0.22 μm organic membrane, and stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for subsequent experiments. As a comparison, the Pi in frozen samples was also tested using the ammonium molybdate spectrophotometry method.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 , the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images clearly indicated that both of these as-prepared nanomaterials were quasi-spherical nanoparticles with well mono-dispersibility. In detail, the average particle size of Y-CDs was about 3.56 nm, while that of B-CDs was slightly smaller at 3.18 nm. Besides, both CDs had distinct lattice fringes, as evidenced by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) photographs.

The surface functional groups of both CDs were identified using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Fig. 1c). The FTIR spectrum of B-CDs showed the predominant characteristic band at 3193 cm−1 for N–H and/or O–H, and three apparent peaks at 1629 cm−1, 1503 cm−1, and 1315 cm−1 belong to the stretching and bending vibrations of CO, N–H, and C–N. The bands at 1149 cm−1 and 748 cm1 were assigned to C–O and –NH2. The characteristic peaks of O–H, N–H, CO, CC, and C–N were found in the FTIR spectrum of Y-CDs, at 3393, 2937, 1654, 1553, and 1249 cm−1, respectively [35,36]. The presence of polar functional groups enables them to be well dispersed in aqueous solutions for corresponding coordination binding, and the abundance of nitrogen-and oxygen-containing groups on the surface of B-CDs also creates the possibility of attaching to terbium ions on Y-CDs. Fig. 1d showed the results of zeta potential experiments performed to describe the surface charges of both CDs. CDs have a negative charge (−1.42 mV), while Tb-CDs have a positive charge (28.17 mV), both of which contribute to the production of B/Y-CDs based on electrostatic attraction.

Fig. 1e showed the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey scan of both CDs, further investigating the chemical compositions. In the full survey XPS spectrum of B-CDs, three main peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s were observed at 285.13, 401.62, and 532.03 eV, respectively, with atomic percentages of 65.7 % (C), 15 % (N), and 19.3 % (O), in that order. The high resolution of the C1s spectrum revealed the presence of C–C/CC, C–N and O–CO groups. The typical N1s spectra have characteristic peaks at 399.55 and 400.26 eV due to C–N and N–O, and the high-resolution of O1s also confirms the existence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in B-CDs (Fig. S1), which is consistent with the FTIR results. Y-CDs consisted mainly of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and, terbium with atomic percentages of 60 %, 16 %, 21.6 %, and 1.8 %, respectively, confirming the successful doping of Terbium. Further analysis of individual elements (Fig. 1f) showed that terbium was present in the form of Tb3+ and Tb4+. The abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on B-CDs and the presence of Tb3+ on Y-CDs provided a basis for the construction of B/Y-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties and photostability of B/Y-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of B-CDs, Y-CDs and B/Y-CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 a, two characteristic absorption peaks can be observed for B-CDs. The CC/CN chromophore's π-π* leap chromophore could be the source of the absorption peak at 241 nm. The n-π* leap in the CO bond may be responsible for the other absorption peak with a center wavelength of 355 nm. The absorption peak of Y-CDs at 420 nm was attributed to the π-π* leap in CC [37]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of B/Y-CDs was similar to this of Y-CDs due to the weaker UV absorption of CDs at the build-up ratio.

B-CDs exhibited the maximum emission peak at 440 nm under 350 nm excitation, and the maximum emission peak of Y-CDs was 560 nm under 420 nm excitation (Fig. S2). The emission peak positions of both CDs remained relatively stable, indicating that the CDs were more consistent in particle size after purification. Notably, the characteristic fluorescent peaks of B-CDs and Y-CDs can be triggered under 365 nm excitation, so we chose the most common excitation wavelength (365 nm) as the excitation wavelength for fluorescence spectrophotometer analysis. Fig. 2b showed the fluorescent properties of the B/Y-CDs for the determination of Pi under 365 nm excitation, as well as those of the B-CDs and Y-CDs. The B-CDs solution was intensely blue under UV light and Y-CDs showed a bright yellow color, while the B/Y-CDs formed after the mixing of the two, it can be seen from the fluorescence spectra that the fluorescence of B-CDs was partially quenching after the combination, whereas the fluorescence of Y-CDs remained relatively stable, further demonstrating that the B/Y-CDs were successfully prepared, providing a basis for the construction of ratiometric fluorescent probes. Moreover, when B-CDs was mixed with Y-CDs to fabricate B/Y-CDs, the position of the emission peak of Y-CDs was adjusted to 550 nm due to the effect of the emission peak of B-CDs. But the large emission spacing of 110 nm between 440 nm and 550 nm still indicated the potential for ratiometric analysis applications. Fig. 2c shows the CIE chromaticity coordinates corresponding to the spectra (Fig. 2b), which were basically consistent with the color observed by the naked eye. Slight deviation was due to camera angle and screen color rendering issues.

In addition, comparing to the yellow emissive CDs without terbium doping, the fluorescence quantum yields of Y-CDs increased from 15.66 % to 28.32 % (Table S1), and a slight red-shift of the fluorescence emission peaks was observed (Fig. S3). The possible reason for this phenomenon is the lower electronegativity of rare earth ions, and dopants with lower electronegativity will tend to redshift the fluorescence spectrum due to the electron donor effect and lower photoexcitation energy [30]. As can be seen, in addition to offering the precise binding sites for the target, terbium doping can also enhance the optical properties of the CDs surprisingly.

An essential presumption for B/Y-CDs' potential practical usage is their photostability. The stability of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F550/F440) under the effects of pH and UV radiation was therefore further investigated. The measured F550/F440 of B/Y-CDs excited at 365 nm were found to essentially stay stable as the pH value increased, as shown in Fig. 3 , confirming their great photostability in environments with extreme pH environments. Furthermore, during 120 min of continuous exposure to a xenon light, the value of F550/F440 remained steady, demonstrating their exceptional anti-photobleaching performance.

Scheme 1 depicted the design strategy of B/Y-CDs fluorescent probe and the working principle of Pi detection. The B-CDs rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups were prepared according to previous reports, and terbium nitrate and o-phenylenediamine were used as precursors in a one-step solvothermal procedure to synthesize the Y-CDs. Numerous previous reports have demonstrated that the rare earth ions doped on the surface of CDs still have coordination properties [23,38], and secondly, the rare earth ions have coordination affinity for oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups, which provides a basis for the binding between B-CDs and Y-CDs. In addition, zeta potential experiments showed that B-CDs were negatively charged and Y-CDs were positively charged, which also contributed to the construction of B/Y-CDs based on electrostatic attraction. More importantly, it can be seen from the fluorescence spectra that the fluorescence of B-CDs was partially quenching after the combination, whereas the fluorescence of Y-CDs remained relatively stable (Fig. 2b). The Y-CDs have a wide absorption spectrum, which overlaps with the fluorescent emission of B-CDs, as shown in Fig. 4 a, indicating that the attenuation of B-CDs fluorescence was likely to be related to the occurrence of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or internal filtration effect (IFE) in B/Y-CDs [39,40]. The change in fluorescence lifetime of quantum dots can be used as an effective method to distinguish between FRET or IFE [41], so experiments of fluorescence lifetime decay were used to further confirm this. The results in Fig. 4b indicated that the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs was significantly shortened (from 4.16 ns to 2.75 ns) after binding to Y-CDs, suggesting that FRET may be the cause of the B-CDs fluorescence quenching.

The investigation of fluorescent spectrum of Y-CDs without terbium doping further confirms the above mechanism. As can be seen in Fig. S4, when Y-CDs without terbium doping was mixed with B-CDs, the spectrum presented was a simple summation of the spectra of the two, and no obvious fluorescence burst of B-CDs was observed, indicating that the combination of the two CDs was no longer feasible without the selective binding site. Therefore, the FRET process can't be achieved.

As seen in Fig. 2b, the blue fluorescence recovered with the addition of Pi. Pi is a common ligand of rare earth ions, and rare earth nanomaterials have been employed in numerous experiments to detect Pi [42,43]. Since the affinity of rare-earth ions for Pi is greater than that of B-CDs, the successful competitive coordination effect of Pi causes the binding between B-CDs and Y-CDs to be broken and the FRET process to be interrupted, and B-CDs gradually break away from Y-CDs, allowing the blue fluorescence to be gradually restored.

Further, the ratio of B-CDs to Y-CDs, pH, and reaction time were all optimized to obtain the best reaction conditions. As seen in Fig. 5 a, B-CDs and Y-CDs were combined in the volume ratios of 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, and 1:5. After adding different ratios of Y-CDs, the blue fluorescence belonging to B-CDs gradually decreased, and basically reached the maximum when the ratio was 1:4, then the fluorescence of B-CDs was no longer significantly weakened by increasing the addition of Y-CDs. So, we selected the volume ratio of 1:4 to fabricate B/Y-CDs. Fig. 5b depicted the influence of pH on the detecting results in the pH range of 4–9. When the pH was 7, the fluorescence recovery efficiency of the system reached the maximum, so the pH of 7 was therefore chosen for the subsequent experiment. In addition, the effect of reaction time on the detection results was also studied (Fig. 5c). The value of F550/F440 no longer increased and reached an equilibrium state after the addition of Pi for 15 min. Therefore, the incubation time for the subsequent experiments was determined to be 15 min.

Under the ideal experimental circumstances, the fluorescence response caused by various Pi concentrations was assessed. Fig. 6 a showed that the fluorescence of the detecting system at 440 nm was significantly enhanced as the Pi concentration increased, while the fluorescence at 550 nm was relatively stable. The linearity of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F550/F440) data versus Pi concentration was in the range of 0.5–12 μM (Fig. 6b). The linear equation can be expressed as Y = 1.1143X+2.5845, where X is the Pi concentration in μM, Y is the fluorescence intensity ratio (F550/F440), and the correlation coefficient is 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD), determined by 3σ/κ where σ is the standard deviation of 11 parallel determinations when Pi has not been added to the detection system, and κ is the slope of the linear equation, was 0.41 μM. Table S2 compared the proposed fluorescent probe with some previously reported fluorescent methods for Pi measurement. Although the linear range and LOD of B/Y-CDs fluorescent probe are not the best, it indeed has the advantages of simple preparation and convenient detection, suggesting the potential application in practical analysis.

Soluble phosphate ions mainly consist of orthophosphate ions, which present in four distinct species depending on the ambient pH: H3PO4, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, and PO4 3−. And at every pH, there are only two or fewer orthophosphate forms [44]. Under neutral conditions, the predominant forms of Pi are H2PO4 − and HPO4 2−. Other types of soluble reactive phosphate ions gradually break down into orthophosphate ions through hydrolysis. In practical sample assays to measure total phosphorus content, other types of phosphate ions can also be converted into orthophosphate ions through pretreatment. We examined the impact of 17 ions and food ingredients with a relatively high coexistence possibility in food samples on the detection system in order to assess the selectivity and anti-interference of the probe. The results demonstrated (Fig. 7 ) that, despite the concentration of these potential coexisting species (20 μM) being several times higher than that of Pi (8 μM), the probe's fluorescence did not change considerably under the identical detection conditions. Additionally, the overall fluorescence intensity ratio did not significantly change when the potential coexisting substances were mixed with B/Y-CDs + Pi as opposed to the B/Y-CDs + Pi control. This selectivity appears to result from the particular way that Tb3+ binds to Pi., and these experimental results suggest the potential of B/Y-CDs to detect real samples. In addition, high concentrations of Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ have varying degrees of influence on the fluorescence system (Fig. S5), so masking agents can be used if necessary.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing of Pi in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To test the Pi sensing ability of the B/Y-CDs probe in real food samples, the detecting results used the standard method (the traditional molybdenum blue colorimetry) [7] were also obtained to make a comparison. The limit of phosphate additives in the national standard is calculated by orthophosphate ions, so in practical sample assays, phosphate additives can also be converted into orthophosphate ions through pretreatment to measure total phosphorus content, which is also a routine practice in the standard method. In this study, three kinds of frozen seafoods (frozen shrimp, frozen fish, and frozen squid) were tested simultaneously by the present probe and standard method after the same pre-treatment, and the unit of Pi concentration was converted to g/kg. As shown in Table 1 , the concentration of Pi in three kinds of frozen seafoods was measured to 0.99 g/kg, 3.18 g/kg, and 1.20 g/kg respectively, which agreed with the results obtained using the standard method (P > 0.05). The Pi concentration in these real samples were less than 5 g/kg, which met the limits of the Chinese national standard. In brief, the testing results in real samples indicate the feasibility of this methodology for food safety monitoring of phosphate additives in the future.

In summary, we developed a novel ratiometric probe for the monitoring of Pi. For the first time, the terbium-doped carbon dots with yellow emissive (Y-CDs) were synthesized and employed to detect Pi. The probe was fabricated by mixing Y-CDs with the blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs). The strong coordination ability of Tb3+ and electrostatic attraction guarantee the close proximity of Y-CDs to B-CDs, leading to the fluorescence quenching of the B-CDs (a donor) by Y-CDs (an acceptor) via the FRET process. In the presence of Pi, the stronger coordination ability with terbium would make the Y-CDs further from the B-CDs, causing the blue fluorescence recovery. Meanwhile, Y-CDs fluorescence remains stable, to achieve the ratiometric detection of Pi. The assay exhibited excellent sensitivity towards Pi with a linear relationship between 0.5 μM and 12 μM and a LOD of 0.41 μM. Additionally, this fluorescent sensor has been further demonstrated to be capable of detecting Pi in real frozen seafood samples. On account of the simple synthesis process and excellent sensing ability, the proposed B/Y-CDs probe offers excellent potential as a novel rapid detecting method in phosphate additives inspecting applications.
Xiaolin Tian: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zhefeng Fan: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Zhefeng fan, xiaolin tian reports financial support, administrative support, and equipment, drugs, or supplies were provided by Shanxi Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111935.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A potentiometric phosphate ion sensor based on electrochemically modified nickel electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: XU K, WU B, WAN J, et al. A Potentiometric Phosphate Ion Sensor Based on Electrochemically Modified Nickel Electrode [J]. Electrochimica Acta, 2022, 412.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitative assessment of phosphate food additive in frozen and chilled chicken using spectrophotometric approach combined with graphitic digestion
